children.name: Acanthiza
children.description: The thornbills — a genus of small, active insectivores with fine, pointed bills and a habit of flicking their wings while foraging. They are the most species-rich genus in the family, occupying virtually every terrestrial habitat across Australasia. Their calls are typically high-pitched and insect-like, and their social structures range from monogamous pairs to complex cooperative groups.

children.name: Acanthiza chrysorrhoa
children.description: A small, active thornbill with a striking yellow rump, a white eyebrow, and finely streaked underparts, widespread across Australia. It is one of the most conspicuous thornbills, often seen in open woodlands and grasslands, where it forages on the ground and in low vegetation. Yellow-rumped Thornbills are highly social, foraging in flocks and often associating with other insectivorous birds.

children.name: Acanthiza pusilla
children.description: A small, nondescript brown thornbill with a rufous forehead, a finely streaked breast, and a thin, high-pitched call, common in the forests and woodlands of eastern and southeastern Australia. It is an active, acrobatic forager, constantly gleaning insects from foliage and bark in the understorey. Its simple but musical song is a series of high-pitched notes and trills.

children.name: Acanthiza lineata
children.description: A tiny, olive-green thornbill with a finely streaked crown and a distinctive brown ear-patch, found in eucalypt forests of eastern and southern Australia. It forages primarily in the canopy, gleaning insects from leaves and bark in fast-moving, restless flocks. Its call is a high-pitched, metallic twittering, and it is a core member of mixed-species feeding flocks.

children.name: Sericornis
children.description: The scrubwrens — a genus of secretive, primarily ground-dwelling warblers with a furtive, mouse-like behaviour, found in the densest parts of forests and heathlands. They have short, rounded wings and strong legs, and their plumage is typically a rich blend of browns, olive-greens, and rufous. Most species are restricted to Australia and New Guinea.

children.name: Sericornis frontalis
children.description: A small, skulking bird with a distinctive white eyebrow, a dark ear-patch, and a rich, bubbling song that emerges from the densest thickets, common throughout coastal eastern and southern Australia. It forages on or near the ground, methodically turning over leaves in search of invertebrates.

children.name: Sericornis humilis
children.description: A dull olive-brown scrubwren with a pale throat and eyebrow, endemic to Tasmania and the Bass Strait islands. It inhabits dense, moist understorey in rainforests, wet eucalypt forests, and temperate heathlands. It is more secretive than its mainland relatives, keeping to the darkest, dampest thickets.

children.name: Smicrornis
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Weebill, which at 8–9 cm is one of the smallest birds in Australia. It is a compact, active insectivore with a short tail, a pale, slightly curved bill, and a persistent, loud call that seems far too big for such a tiny bird. It is common across much of the continent in dry forests and woodlands.

children.name: Smicrornis brevirostris
children.description: Australia's smallest bird, a tiny olive-grey warbler with a short, stubby bill and a remarkably loud, piping call that carries long distances through the eucalypt canopy. It feeds exclusively on insects and spiders, gleaning them from the outer foliage of trees in the mid-to-upper canopy. It is a common but often overlooked resident of dry woodlands across mainland Australia.

children.name: Cincloramphus
children.description: A genus of large, grassland-dwelling warblers found across Australasia and the Pacific. They are robust, streaky birds with strong legs, adapted for a terrestrial lifestyle in open habitats. Their songs are often loud and complex, delivered from exposed perches or in flight.

children.name: Cincloramphus cruralis
children.description: A large, plain brown songbird with a heavily streaked breast and a long, rounded tail, found across grasslands and open woodlands of Australia. Males sing a complex, jumbled song from exposed perches or in a soaring display flight. It is nomadic, moving in response to rainfall and grasshopper abundance.

children.name: Cincloramphus timoriensis
children.description: A medium-sized grassbird with warm tawny-brown upperparts, streaked darker, and a pale throat and belly. It ranges from the Philippines through Indonesia to New Guinea and northern Australia. It inhabits grasslands, reed beds, and marsh edges.

children.name: Cincloramphus mathewsi
children.description: A heavily streaked brown grassbird with a distinctive pale eyebrow and a long, graduated tail, endemic to Australia. It inhabits dense grasslands, swamps, and reed beds across northern and eastern Australia. Its song is a series of scratchy, buzzing notes.

children.name: Poodytes
children.description: A genus of small, secretive grass-dwelling warblers endemic to Australia and New Zealand. They are skulking birds of dense grassland and swamp vegetation, with short tails and strong legs adapted for a terrestrial lifestyle.

children.name: Poodytes gramineus
children.description: A small, skulking grassbird with streaked brown upperparts, a pale throat, and a distinctive musical, descending song that sounds like a silver whistle. It is found across Australia and southern New Guinea in dense swamp vegetation, reed beds, and wet grasslands. It is far more often heard than seen.

children.name: Poodytes carteri
children.description: A small, plain brown bird with a pale eyebrow and a long tail, endemic to arid and semi-arid regions of inland Australia. It is closely associated with spinifex grasslands and Triodia hummocks, where it skulks secretively. Its song is a rapid, high-pitched twittering.

children.name: Gerygone
children.description: The gerygones — a genus of small, insectivorous warblers found from Southeast Asia through Australasia to the Pacific islands. They are active, acrobatic foragers with fine, pointed bills and often brightly coloured facial patterns. Many species are known for their distinctive, descending songs, which give them their common name 'flyeaters'.

children.name: Gerygone fusca
children.description: A small, pale grey warbler with a white eyebrow and throat, found across much of Australia in dry eucalypt forests and woodlands. It is an active forager, gleaning insects from the outer foliage of trees. Its song is a distinctive, descending whistle.

children.name: Gerygone palpebrosa
children.description: A brightly coloured gerygone with a yellow face, olive-green upperparts, and a white throat, found in New Guinea and northeastern Australia. It inhabits rainforests and mangroves, foraging actively in the canopy. Its sweet, descending song is a characteristic sound of its habitat.

children.name: Gerygone magnirostris
children.description: A small warbler with a notably stout bill, olive-grey upperparts, and pale underparts, found from Southeast Asia through New Guinea to northern Australia. It inhabits mangroves and coastal forests, foraging for insects in the foliage. Its song is a series of clear, whistled notes.

children.name: Gerygone levigaster
children.description: A pale grey warbler with a white throat and a prominent white eyebrow, found in mangroves and coastal forests from Southeast Asia to northern Australia. It is closely associated with mangrove habitats, where it forages actively for insects. Its song is a thin, descending trill.

children.name: Gerygone mouki
children.description: A small, brownish warbler with a pale throat and a faint pale eyebrow, found in rainforests and wet sclerophyll forests of eastern Australia. It is an active, acrobatic forager in the forest canopy. Its song is a high-pitched, descending trill.

children.name: Gerygone cinerea
children.description: A plain grey warbler with a pale throat and belly, found in the montane forests of New Guinea. It forages actively in the canopy, often associating with mixed-species flocks. Its song is a series of high-pitched, sibilant notes.

children.name: Gerygone chloronota
children.description: A small warbler with bright olive-green upperparts, a grey crown, and a yellow wash on the underparts, found in New Guinea and the Top End of Australia. It inhabits monsoon forests, mangroves, and dense woodlands. Its song is a sweet, descending warble.
